feedingProfile.preferredNpk: low-nitrogen / high-potassium (e.g. 5-10-10 in spring; high-K liquid feed for containers)

commonIssues: In-ground cherry trees reach 4.5-9 m tall; container culture requires a dwarfing rootstock such as 'Gisela 5' and a pot at least 45-50 cm wide and deep (~90 L). The recommendedMinLiters reflects this minimum dwarf-container size, not a full standard tree, which must be grown in the ground.
No tolerance for wet or poorly drained soils; overwatering and waterlogging cause root rot and crown gall.
Heavy rain or excessive irrigation near harvest causes fruit cracking/splitting.
Spring frosts during blossom can destroy the crop; protect flowers from late frosts.
Disease-prone: bacterial canker, brown rot, cherry leaf spot/scorch, silver leaf, black knot, powdery mildew, shot-hole.
Pests: birds and squirrels strip fruit (use netting), plus aphids, spotted wing drosophila / cherry fruit flies, peach tree borer, scale.

Self-fertile cultivars (e.g. 'Stella', 'Lapins', 'Sweetheart') fruit alone, but most older sweet cherries need a compatible pollinizer that blooms at the same time, planted within ~15-30 m; even self-fertile types crop more heavily with a partner.
Requires roughly 800-1200 winter chill hours below ~7 C to break dormancy and fruit.

source.citation: Missouri Botanical Garden, Plant Finder: Prunus avium — USDA zones 3-8, full sun to part shade, medium moisture, no tolerance for wet soils, white flowers in April, small sweet red-to-black cherries ripen early summer; lists leaf spot, bacterial canker, brown rot, black knot, crown gall, leaf curl/scorch, powdery mildew, root rot, scab, shot-hole, plus aphids/fruit flies/sawflies/scale and bird/squirrel pressure.
Missouri Botanical Garden, Plant Finder: Prunus avium — full sun to part shade; well-drained soil with no tolerance for wet soils.
Missouri Botanical Garden, Plant Finder: Prunus avium — extensive disease/pest list and note that cold winter temperatures and spring frosts cause significant injury to buds/flowers.

RHS, How to grow cherries — sunny south/south-west position; deep, fertile, well-drained slightly acidic soil pH 6.5-6.7; water newly planted trees in dry spells for the first year, established trees generally need no extra water; avoid overwatering in summer (causes fruit splitting); feed established mulched trees little, or granular fertiliser in early spring; container trees fed fortnightly with high-potassium liquid feed April-August.
RHS, How to grow cherries — feeding regime: granular fertiliser in early spring; containers fortnightly high-potassium liquid feed April-August; prune after harvest (late July/August).
RHS, How to grow cherries — container growing possible on semi-dwarfing rootstock 'Gisela 5' in a pot at least 45-50 cm (18-20 in) wide and deep using loam-based compost.

RHS, How to grow cherries — many modern sweet cherries are self-fertile; others need a compatible partner flowering at the same time; ripens early summer onwards; pests/diseases include birds, silver leaf, bacterial canker, brown rot, leaf scorch, spotted wing drosophila.

RHS, Prunus avium 'Stella' (F) — self-fertile sweet cherry; full sun, south/west aspect; RHS hardiness H6 (-20 to -15 C); cropping season late July; reaches 2.5-4 m in 5-10 years; fruit prone to splitting in wet weather.
RHS, Prunus avium 'Stella' (F) — self-fertile cultivar example; late-July cropping; spring white flowers.

Penn State Extension, Cherries in the Garden and the Kitchen — full sun; USDA zones 5-9; most sweet cherries need a different compatible cultivar nearby though several newer cultivars are self-fruitful (all crop better with a second cultivar); sweet and tart cherries cannot pollinate each other; diseases include cherry leaf spot, powdery mildew, rhizopus rot, bacterial canker; pests include cherry/black cherry fruit flies, peach tree borer, plum curculio, birds and mammals; fruit cracks with excessive rainfall near harvest.

UC IPM (UC ANR), Cultural Tips for Growing Cherry — full sun, good air movement, well-drained slightly acidic sandy loam at least 4 ft deep, no tolerance for poorly drained soil; young trees up to 3-5 gallons of water per week; established trees irrigated deeply every 2-3 weeks (12-24 h) rather than frequent shallow watering; apply nitrogen annually in the growing season (mature trees ~3-5 lb ammonium sulfate or 2 lb urea); cherries require 800-1200 chill hours below 45 F.
UC IPM (UC ANR), Cultural Tips for Growing Cherry — nitrogen applied annually during the growing season; chill requirement 800-1200 hours below 45 F.

Confidence note: baseIntervalDays (watering=10, feeding=14) and hotWeatherMultiplier/rainSkipThresholdMm are conservative app-cadence values interpolated from qualitative guidance. Sources give young-tree weekly watering (UC IPM 3-5 gal/week) and established deep watering every 2-3 weeks (UC IPM), with established in-ground trees often needing no supplemental water (RHS); 10 days is a middle-ground default for a managed/container tree. Feeding 14 days follows RHS fortnightly container feeding; in-ground feeding is effectively annual in early spring. heatStressC (35) is an estimate — no source gave an explicit upper-temperature stress threshold. verticalSuitability is low (tree habit, not trellis/hanging).
